cards.grammarNote:
**doler** (to hurt) works like **gustar** — the body part is the grammatical subject
**Me duele la cabeza** (singular) · **Me duelen los pies** (plural)
The pronoun **me/te/le** indicates who is hurting

**el pelo** is the everyday word for hair (on the head); **el cabello** is more formal/literary.
In the phrase **Ella tiene el pelo largo**, the definite article **el** is used instead of a possessive — Spanish describes physical characteristics this way: **tiene los ojos azules** (she has blue eyes), **tiene la nariz pequeña** (he has a small nose).

**Lávate la cara** is a reflexive command (tú form): **lávate** = wash yourself.
With body-care reflexive verbs, use the **definite article** (**la cara**, **las manos**) not a possessive (**tu cara**).
The reflexive pronoun **te** already shows whose face it is.
Formal command (usted): **Lávese la cara**.

Paired body parts use the plural with the definite article: **los ojos** (eyes), **las manos** (hands), **los pies** (feet), **los oídos** (ears).
Spanish describes eye color with **tener + los + body part + adjective**: **Él tiene los ojos azules** — literally 'he has the eyes blue.' The adjective follows the noun and agrees in gender and number.

Spanish has two words for ear: **la oreja** (the outer, visible ear) and **el oído** (the inner ear / sense of hearing).
**Me duele la oreja** = my outer ear hurts · **Tengo el oído tapado** = my inner ear is blocked.
**doler** works like **gustar**: the body part is the subject, **me** is the indirect object pronoun.

**Tengo la nariz fría** uses **tener + definite article + body part + adjective** to describe a physical state.
This pattern is very common: **tengo el cuello rígido** (my neck is stiff), **tengo la pierna cansada** (my leg is tired).
Contrast with **me duele** for pain: **me duele la nariz** (my nose hurts).

**Abre la boca** is an informal (tú) imperative. Regular -ar verbs form the tú command with the él/ella present form: **abr-e**.
The formal (usted) command is **Abra la boca** — note the vowel switch: -ar verbs use -e for formal commands.
Note the definite article **la** — not *tu boca*.

Paired body parts use the plural with definite article: **los labios** (lips), not *sus labios* when describing a person's physical features.
**Tiene los labios rojos** — the adjective **rojos** agrees in gender (masculine) and number (plural) with **labios**.
This **tener + los/las + body part + adjective** structure describes physical appearance.

**doler** works like **gustar** — the tooth is the grammatical subject: **Me duele un diente** (a tooth is hurting me).
Note **un** (indefinite article) instead of **el** here — 'a tooth' (one in particular, but not previously specified).
For general toothache: **tener dolor de muelas** (to have a toothache) — **muela** refers specifically to a molar.

**la lengua** means both 'tongue' (body part) and 'language' in Spanish: **hablar una lengua** (to speak a language).
**Saca la lengua** — **sacar** (to take out / stick out) is an informal tú command. Note the definite article **la** instead of a possessive.
For the formal command: **Saque la lengua**.

**el cuello** means both 'neck' (body part) and 'collar/neckline' of a garment.
**Tengo el cuello rígido** uses **tener + el + body part + adjective** for physical states.
For pain: **Me duele el cuello** (my neck hurts) using the **doler** + gustar pattern.
**rígido** (stiff/rigid) agrees in gender with **cuello** (masculine).

**Me rompí el brazo** — reflexive verb (**romper** → **romperse**) with the definite article, not a possessive.
The reflexive pronoun **me** shows it's my own arm; **el** replaces *mi*.
This pattern applies to all injury verbs: **me torcí el tobillo** (I sprained my ankle), **me corté el dedo** (I cut my finger).
**rompí** is preterite (past): **romper** → rompí, rompiste, rompió, rompimos, rompieron.

With reflexive verbs and body parts, use the definite article instead of a possessive:
**Lávate las manos** (not *lava tus manos*) · **Me rompí el brazo** (I broke my arm)
The reflexive pronoun **me/te/se** makes clear whose body part it is

Spanish uses **el dedo** for both finger and toe. To distinguish:
**el dedo de la mano** (finger) · **el dedo del pie** (toe).
**Se cortó el dedo** — reflexive **cortarse** (to cut oneself) with definite article instead of possessive.
The reflexive pronoun **se** shows it was his own finger; **el** replaces *su*.

**tener dolor en** + body part = to have pain in a body part: **tiene dolor en el pecho** (he has chest pain).
Alternative: **tener dolor de** + body part for common ailments: **dolor de cabeza** (headache), **dolor de espalda** (back pain), **dolor de estómago** (stomachache).
**el pecho** can also refer to the breast area generally; **el seno** is more specific for breast.
cards.phrase: Él tiene dolor en el pecho.

cards.grammarNote:
**doler** works like **gustar**: **Me duele la espalda** — literally 'the back hurts to me.'
For specific regions: **la parte baja de la espalda** (lower back) · **la parte alta de la espalda** (upper back).
Common compound: **dolor de espalda** (back pain) — used with **tener**: **tengo dolor de espalda**.

**corazón** comes from Latin **cor** (heart) — the same root as English 'courage,' 'cordial,' 'core,' and 'accord.'
**late** (beats/throbs) comes from **latir** — used specifically for heartbeat: **el corazón late rápido**.
Note the possessive **Mi corazón** here — used because this is a statement about the subject's own heart without a reflexive verb.

**doler** works like **gustar**: **Me duele el estómago** — the stomach is the grammatical subject.
Common alternatives: **tener dolor de estómago** · **tener el estómago revuelto** (to have an upset stomach).
Note: **el estómago** (stomach organ) vs. **el vientre/la barriga** (belly, more colloquial).

**Me jalé un músculo** — **jalarse** (to pull, strain) is primarily used in Latin America; in Spain the equivalent is **me estiré un músculo**.
The reflexive **me** shows the action happened to the speaker's own body; **un** (not **el**) because it's 'a muscle' — one unspecified one.
For generalized soreness: **tengo los músculos doloridos** (my muscles are sore).

**Ella tiene la cintura delgada** — **tener + la + body part + adjective** describes physical features.
The adjective **delgada** agrees in gender (feminine) with **la cintura**.
Related: **la talla de cintura** (waist size) · **ceñir** (to cinch/hug the waist) — **cintura** shares its Latin root with English 'cincture.'

**Ella tiene la piel suave** — **tener + la + body part + adjective** for physical descriptions.
**la piel** (skin) also means leather/hide — a shared literal meaning: **una chaqueta de piel** (a leather jacket).
With **estar**: **estar en la piel de alguien** = to be in someone's shoes (idiomatic).
**suave** is invariable for gender — same form for masculine and feminine.

**la sangre** (blood) is feminine — used without article after **donar**: **donó sangre** (donated blood), like **donar tiempo** (to donate time).
Related vocabulary: **sangrar** (to bleed) · **el análisis de sangre** (blood test) · **la presión arterial** (blood pressure).
**sangriento** (bloody) and **ensangrentado** (bloodied) are common adjectives.

**cerebro** comes from Latin **cerebrum** — directly related to English 'cerebral' and 'cerebellum' (the little brain).
With **ser** (permanent characteristic): **El cerebro es complejo** — use **ser** for inherent properties of things.
Contrast: **estar** would suggest a temporary state — **el cerebro está activo** (the brain is [currently] active).

**el cráneo** comes from Greek **kranion** — related to English 'cranium' and 'cranial.'
**protege** (protects) — from **proteger**, a -ger verb: the **g** softens to **j** before **o/a**: **protejo** (I protect), but **proteges, protege, protegemos**.
**El cráneo protege el cerebro** — both nouns use definite articles for generic reference (the skull as a concept).

**el seno** (breast) — also used in the plural **los senos**. **el pecho** is used more broadly for chest/breast area.
**sintió** is the preterite of **sentir** (to feel) — an e→i stem-changing verb in the preterite: **sentí, sentiste, sintió, sentimos, sentisteis, sintieron**.
**tener dolor en** + body part: **tuvo dolor en el pecho** (she had chest pain).
cards.englishPhrase: She felt pain in her chest.
cards.phrase: Ella sintió dolor en el pecho.

cards.grammarNote:
**Tengo la pierna cansada** — **tener + la + body part + adjective** for physical states.
The adjective **cansada** agrees in gender (feminine) with **la pierna**.
For pain: **Me duele la pierna** (my leg hurts) — using the **doler** + gustar pattern.
Paired form: **tengo las piernas cansadas** (my legs are tired) — plural adjective agrees with plural noun.

Paired body parts use the plural: **los pies** (feet) — **Me duelen los pies** (my feet hurt).
**doler** agrees with the plural subject: **me duele** (singular) · **me duelen** (plural).
Common expression: **estar de pie** (to be standing) · **ponerse de pie** (to stand up) · **a pie** (on foot).

**el dedo del pie** (toe) — **del** is a contraction of **de + el**. This distinguishes toe from finger (**el dedo de la mano**).
**Se golpeó el dedo del pie** — reflexive **golpearse** with definite article: the reflexive **se** shows it was his own toe.
**golpear** (to hit/strike) → **golpearse** (to hit/bang oneself). Preterite: **golpeó**.
cards.phrase: Se golpeó el dedo del pie.

cards.grammarNote:
**Tengo las articulaciones rígidas** — **tener + las + body part (plural) + adjective** for physical states.
The adjective **rígidas** agrees in gender (feminine) and number (plural) with **las articulaciones**.
**la articulación** also means 'articulation' in speech/language — related English cognate: 'articulate.'
Related: **articular** (to articulate) · **articulado** (articulated/jointed).

Note the vocabulary distinction: **el aliento** (breath as air breathed out, including breath odor) · **la respiración** (the act/process of breathing).
The phrase uses **la respiración** rather than **el aliento**: **Aguanta la respiración** (hold your breath — the act of breathing).
**aguantar** (to hold, to bear, to endure) — tú command: **aguanta**. Also used: **tener mal aliento** (to have bad breath).

**ver** (to see) is irregular in the present: **veo, ves, ve, vemos, veis, ven**.
**Puedo ver con claridad** — **poder + infinitive** (can/to be able to + verb).
**poder** is an o→ue stem-changer: **puedo, puedes, puede, podemos, podéis, pueden**.
Distinction: **ver** (to see, passive perception) vs. **mirar** (to look at, active attention): **Mira** ese pájaro · **Veo** la luna.

Spanish has two verbs for hearing: **oír** (to hear, passive) and **escuchar** (to listen, active).
The phrase uses **escucho** — 'I'm not listening to you / I can't hear you' (active attention).
**No te escucho** — **te** is an indirect object pronoun here ('you' as the one being heard).
**oír** is highly irregular: **oigo, oyes, oye, oímos, oís, oyen**.

**oler** (to smell) is an o→hue stem-changing verb — uniquely, it adds an **h** before the diphthong: **huelo, hueles, huele, olemos, oléis, huelen**.
This spelling preserves the /w/ sound — Spanish doesn't begin a syllable with 'ue' without 'h' when it could be confused.
**Puedo oler las flores** — **poder + infinitive** for ability.

Two verbs for tasting: **saborear** (to savor, taste slowly) and **probar** (to taste/try).
The phrase uses **Prueba** (try/taste this) — an informal tú command of **probar** (o→ue stem-changer).
**probar** also means 'to try on' (clothing) and 'to prove': **¿Probaste el vestido?** (Did you try on the dress?)
**saborear** is from **sabor** (flavor/taste) — related to 'savor' in English.

**¡No toques eso!** — **toques** is the negative tú subjunctive command (present subjunctive) of **tocar**.
Negative tú commands always use the present subjunctive: **no hables, no comas, no toques**.
**tocar** has a spelling change in the subjunctive: c→qu before **e** to preserve the /k/ sound: **toque, toques, toque**.
**tocar** also means 'to play' (an instrument): **toco la guitarra**.

**Respira despacio** — informal (tú) imperative of **respirar** (regular -ar verb): drop the -r and the form is identical to the third-person present.
Formal command: **Respire despacio** — note vowel switch (-ar → -e ending for usted).
**despacio** (slowly) is an adverb — invariable, never changes form.
Related: **la respiración** (breathing/respiration) · **respiratorio** (respiratory).

**Dobla las rodillas** — informal (tú) imperative of **doblar** (regular -ar).
**la rodilla** (knee) comes from Latin **rotella** (little wheel) — the kneecap's circular shape. Related English cognate: 'rotella' → 'rotula' (medical term for kneecap).
Note definite article **las** instead of possessive — 'bend the knees' (understood as yours).
**doblar** also means 'to fold,' 'to turn' (a corner), and 'to dub' (a film).

**Estira los brazos** — informal (tú) imperative of **estirar** (regular -ar).
Note **los brazos** (the arms) with definite article, not *tus brazos*.
**estirar** vs. **extender**: **estirar** suggests pulling/stretching something out; **extender** (to extend/spread) is used for more formal contexts.
Related: **el estiramiento** (the stretch/stretching) · **estirarse** (to stretch oneself — reflexive).

**doler** works like **gustar**: **Me duele el hombro** — literally 'the shoulder hurts to me.'
Common expressions: **encogerse de hombros** (to shrug — literally 'to shrink from the shoulders') · **cargar con algo sobre los hombros** (to carry something on one's shoulders — literal and figurative).
For injury: **me hice daño en el hombro** or **me lesioné el hombro** (I hurt my shoulder).

**la rodilla** comes from Latin **rotella** (little wheel) — referring to the round kneecap. Related medical term: **la rótula** (kneecap/patella).
**Me lastimé la rodilla** — reflexive **lastimarse** (to hurt oneself) with definite article instead of possessive.
Alternative: **me hice daño en la rodilla** (I hurt myself on the knee) — **hacerse daño en** + body part is a very common injury expression.

**Se golpeó el codo** — reflexive **golpearse** (to bump/hit oneself) with definite article.
The reflexive **se** shows the action happened to his own body; **el** replaces *su*.
**el codo** also appears in the idiom **hablar por los codos** (to talk someone's ear off — literally 'to talk through the elbows').
For pain: **me duele el codo** (my elbow hurts) — **doler** + gustar pattern.

**Flexiona los músculos** — informal (tú) imperative of **flexionar** (regular -ar).
**flexionar** is a direct borrowing from Latin **flexio** (bending) — related to English 'flex,' 'flexible,' 'deflect.'
Note **los músculos** with definite article instead of possessive.
Alternative: **doblar** (to bend/flex) is more colloquial; **flexionar** is the medical/exercise term.

**No parpadees** — negative tú subjunctive command of **parpadear** (regular -ar).
Negative tú commands always use the present subjunctive: no hables, no comas, no **parpadees**.
**parpadear** comes from **el párpado** (eyelid) — the verb literally means 'to do the eyelid thing.'
Related: **el parpadeo** (a blink/blinking) · **parpadear** also describes a flickering light: **la luz parpadeaba** (the light was flickering).

**Sudo cuando corro** — both verbs are present tense, expressing habitual action (I sweat [whenever] I run).
Spanish uses present indicative in **cuando** clauses for habitual/repeated events.
Contrast: for future events, **cuando** takes the subjunctive: **Cuando corra mañana, sudaré** (When I run tomorrow, I'll sweat).
**sudar** (to sweat) · **el sudor** (sweat/perspiration) · **sudoroso** (sweaty).

**La herida está sangrando** — **estar + gerund** for an action in progress right now (present progressive).
The gerund **sangrando** is formed by dropping -ar and adding **-ando**: sangr- + ando.
Contrast with simple present **sangra** (bleeds/is bleeding — habitual or general): **La herida sangra fácilmente** (the wound bleeds easily).
**la herida** (wound/injury) — related: **herir** (to wound/injure), **herido** (wounded).

**¿Cómo te sientes?** uses **sentirse** (reflexive) for emotional/physical feeling: **me siento bien** (I feel well).
Contrast with non-reflexive **sentir** (to feel/sense something): **Siento un dolor** (I feel a pain).
**sentirse** conjugates like a reflexive: **me siento, te sientes, se siente, nos sentimos, os sentís, se sienten**.
**sentir** is an e→ie stem-changer in the present: **siento, sientes, siente**.

**pensar** is an e→ie stem-changing verb — changes in all forms except nosotros/vosotros:
**pienso · piensas · piensa · pensamos · pensáis · piensan**
Other e→ie verbs: **querer** (quiero) · **entender** (entiendo) · **empezar** (empiezo) · **perder** (pierdo)

**mover** is an o→ue stem-changing verb: **muevo, mueves, mueve, movemos, movéis, mueven**.
**No puedo mover el brazo** — note **el brazo** (the arm) with definite article, not *mi brazo*.
**poder** (can/to be able to) is also o→ue: **puedo, puedes, puede**.
Reflexive **moverse** = to move (oneself): **No puedo moverme** (I can't move).

**Tiene las manos huesudas** — **tener + las + body part + adjective** for physical descriptions.
**huesudo** is formed from **el hueso** (bone) + **-udo** suffix (meaning 'having a lot of' something): compare **peludo** (hairy, from pelo), **barbudo** (bearded, from barba), **musculoso** (muscular).
The adjective **huesudas** agrees in gender (feminine) and number (plural) with **las manos**.

**peludo** is formed from **el pelo** (hair) + **-udo** suffix (meaning 'having a lot of'): **peludo** (hairy), **huesudo** (bony), **barbudo** (bearded), **barrigudo** (pot-bellied).
**Él tiene los brazos peludos** — **tener + los + body part (plural) + adjective** for physical description.
**peludos** agrees in gender (masculine) and number (plural) with **los brazos**.

**suave** is invariable for gender — same form for masculine and feminine: **el tejido suave** / **la piel suave**.
**ser** vs. **estar** with **suave**: **El tejido es suave** (the tissue is soft — inherent property, use **ser**) vs. **El tejido está suave** (the tissue feels soft — current/perceived state, use **estar**).
Related: **suavidad** (softness) · **suavizar** (to soften) · **suavemente** (softly, gently).

**Tengo los músculos doloridos** — **tener + los + body part (plural) + adjective** for physical states.
**dolorido** (sore/aching) comes from **dolor** (pain) — related to **doler** (to hurt) and **doloroso** (painful).
The adjective **doloridos** agrees in gender (masculine) and number (plural) with **los músculos**.
Common after exercise: **tengo las piernas doloridas** (my legs are sore).

**izquierdo/izquierda** (left) comes from Basque **ezker** — one of the few Basque loanwords in Spanish.
Adjectives of direction follow the noun: **la rodilla izquierda** (the left knee), **el brazo derecho** (the right arm).
**izquierda** agrees in gender (feminine) with **la rodilla**.
Common expressions: **a la izquierda** (to the left) · **de izquierdas** (left-wing, politically).

**derecho/derecha** (right direction) also means 'straight' and 'right' (legal/moral): **los derechos humanos** (human rights), **ir derecho** (to go straight).
**Me rompí el brazo derecho** — reflexive injury pattern with definite article: the **me** shows whose arm, so **el** not *mi*.
**derecho** agrees in gender (masculine) with **el brazo**.
Distinction: **derecho** (adjective, right/straight) vs. **a la derecha** (to the right — directional phrase).

**superior** is invariable for gender — same form for masculine and feminine nouns.
**la parte superior del cuerpo** — **del** is a contraction of **de + el**: *de el* → **del**.
**ser** is used here (**es fuerte**) because strength is an inherent characteristic of the upper body — not a temporary state.
Opposite: **inferior** (lower) — both are Latin-derived adjectives, same in English.
cards.phrase: La parte superior del cuerpo es fuerte.

cards.grammarNote:
**inferior** is invariable for gender — same form for masculine and feminine nouns.
**tener dolor en la parte inferior de la espalda** — note **en** (in/at) before the specific location; compare **tener dolor de espalda** (general back pain) without **en**.
**del** (de + el) is used for contractions, but here the article is **la** (feminine), so no contraction: **de la espalda**.
Opposite: **superior** (upper) — both invariable adjectives from Latin.
cards.phrase: Él tiene dolor en la parte inferior de la espalda.

cards.grammarNote:
**El cuerpo humano es asombroso** — **ser** is used (**es**) because this describes an inherent, permanent quality.
**asombroso** (amazing/astonishing) comes from **asombrar** (to astonish) — related to English 'umbra' (shadow), from the idea of being 'overshadowed' by awe.
**el cuerpo** appears in many expressions: **en cuerpo y alma** (body and soul) · **a cuerpo de rey** (in the lap of luxury — literally 'at a king's body') · **hacer del cuerpo** (to go to the bathroom, euphemistic).

**humano/humana** (human) is a regular adjective that agrees in gender and number: **el cuerpo humano** · **la biología humana** · **los derechos humanos**.
In **Es parte de la biología humana**, **es** (ser) describes a permanent category — biology is inherently human here.
**humano** comes from Latin **humanus** — related to English 'humane,' 'humanity,' 'humility,' and even 'humble' (all from Latin *humus*, earth — humans as earthly beings).
cards.phrase: Es parte de la biología humana.
